retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Environmental Skin Cancer Risks -- Issues of Concern -- Cigarette Smoke
retrieved_docs.content: Cigarette smoke contains numerous harmful chemicals and has been implicated in cancers affecting the respiratory, gastrointestinal, and genitourinary tracts. Evidence suggests a correlation between cigarette smoke and skin cancer. Cigarette smoking has been associated with an increased risk of squamous cell carcinoma, particularly on the lips, ears, penis, and vulva. [10] The most significant risk is found in heavy smokers and individuals with prolonged exposure. Quitting smoking not only lowers the risk of skin and respiratory cancers but also enhances cardiovascular health.

retrieved_docs.title: Parotid Cancer -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Salivary gland cancer can be prevented by avoiding the possible risk factors namely tobacco and alcohol. For patients who work in certain industries linked with an increased risk of salivary gland cancer, taking precautions to protect themselves might help lower the risk of cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: Smokeless Tobacco Oral Pathology -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The treatment of choice for these lesions is surgical excision with clear margins (normal tissue) to reduce the chance of recurrence. Squamous cell carcinoma should be excised entirely to achieve clear margins and reduce the potential for recurrence. In addition, patients should be counseled on their high-risk behaviors to aid in reducing their risk of recurrence or the development of a new primary lesion.

retrieved_docs.title: Smokeless Tobacco Oral Pathology -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify oral mucosal lesions associated with smokeless tobacco use, including smokeless tobacco keratosis. Assess the clinical presentation and histopathology of oral lesions caused by smokeless tobacco use. Evaluate the potential malignant transformation risk of smokeless tobacco-related oral lesions. Collaborate and communicate effectively within the interprofessional healthcare team to optimize early detection, timely management, and patient education of oral mucosal lesions associated with smokeless tobacco use. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Malignant Salivary Gland Tumors -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The exact etiology of salivary gland cancer is unknown. Still, various mechanisms have been proposed, including radiation, viruses (EBV and HIV), immunosuppression, ultraviolet light exposure, occupational exposures in rubber or nickel industries, prior diagnosis of medulloblastoma, prior diagnosis of basal cell carcinoma, androgen receptor expression, and genetics. [2] [3] [4] [5] [6] [7] [8] In studies involving Japanese survivors of the atomic bomb and patients who received radiation treatment during childhood for benign conditions, radiation exposure was identified as a significant risk factor for the development of salivary malignancies. [9] [10] MEC appears to be the most common salivary gland malignancy associated with radiation exposure. [11] [12] Compared to other head and neck cancers, the risk of developing malignant salivary gland tumors from exposure to tobacco and alcohol has been controversial, with several studies noting both positive association and no appreciable association. Sawabe et al found no significant association between cigarette smoking and MEC but did show a positive correlation between tobacco smoking and other malignant salivary histologies. [13] [14] Chronic inflammation of the salivary glands has not been established as a risk factor. However, autoimmune conditions such as Sjogren syndrome may predispose an individual to develop a salivary gland malignancy such as lymphoma. [15] [16] [17] [18] Although squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) of the head and neck has been linked to tobacco, alcohol, and UV exposure, the presence of primary SCC in the salivary glands is rare and does not retain the same risk factors. [19] The most significant risk factor for primary SCC of the salivary glands appears to be prior gland radiation. [20] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: High Risk Behaviors -- Clinical Significance -- Tobacco Use
retrieved_docs.content: Tobacco use is a major risk factor for the development of lung diseases like bronchitis, emphysema, and idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis. Smoking mediates atherosclerosis, which leads to a variety of cardiac and vascular pathologies like myocardial infarction, stroke, peripheral vascular disease, and erectile dysfunction. Furthermore, cigarette smoke contains many carcinogens responsible for causing various cancers, particularly of the lung, mouth, larynx, esophagus, and pancreas. [25] [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Smokeless Tobacco Oral Pathology -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Verrucous carcinoma should be evaluated closely, as it is a low-grade variant of squamous cell carcinoma and may be easily overlooked and/or misdiagnosed. [21] With varied clinical presentations, squamous cell carcinoma should be evaluated closely. In addition, patients must be interviewed in-depth about their habits to determine if they are at high risk for developing oral cancer. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Oropharyngeal Squamous Cell Carcinoma -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: These professionals are also in an optimal position to influence and advise patients on quitting smoking and limiting alcohol in addition to maintaining healthy lifestyle habits such as eating fruits and vegetables, observing dental hygiene, and having safe sex, which may contribute to preventing the increasing incidence of oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma.

retrieved_docs.title: Smokeless Tobacco Oral Pathology -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Verrucous carcinoma is a low-grade variant of squamous cell carcinoma (see Image . Buccal Vestibule With Hyperkeratosis and Verrucous Carcinoma). While its etiology is not clearly defined, human papillomavirus, smoking, poor oral hygiene, lichenoid lesions, or chewing tobacco have been attributed to its causation. [8] Squamous cell carcinoma has a variety of etiologies, with smoking and alcohol consumption regarded as significant risk factors. This is due to their synergistic effect in the development of malignancy and their associated usage in 90% of all reported cases. [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Melanoma of the Head and Neck -- Etiology -- Melanoma Risks
retrieved_docs.content: Socioeconomic status: Lower socioeconomic status may be linked to more advanced disease at the time of detection.  This may be due to a decreased knowledge of the diseases and decreased perception of risk.

retrieved_docs.title: Smokeless Tobacco Oral Pathology -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Through participation in this activity, clinicians enhance their competence in identifying and evaluating oral mucosal lesions associated with smokeless tobacco use. The course provides targeted instruction on differentiating lesion types, documenting clinical findings, and determining when referral for biopsy or specialist evaluation is warranted. Participants gain skills in counseling patients on the health risks of smokeless tobacco and promoting evidence-based cessation strategies. Emphasis is placed on integrating care within an interprofessional team—collaborating with dental professionals, primary care clinicians, oral surgeons, and oncology specialists—to ensure comprehensive assessment, timely diagnosis, and coordinated management. This collaborative approach supports earlier detection of high-risk lesions, facilitates appropriate intervention, and ultimately improves patient outcomes by reducing the incidence of malignant transformation.

retrieved_docs.title: Ocular Surface Squamous Neoplasia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The etiology of OSSN is multifactorial. Various studies have implicated human papillomavirus (HPV), ultraviolet B (UVB) light rays, human immunodeficiency (HIV) 1 and 2 viruses, and Hepatitis B and C as the most common associations. The various predisposing factors are chronic cigarette smoking, use of petroleum products, hypopigmented hair and eyes, xerophthalmia (vitamin A deficiency), chemicals like arsenic and beryllium, ocular surface trauma, and people from British, Austria, and Switzerland are at high risk. [6] [7] [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Cancer of the Oral Mucosa -- Etiology -- Tobacco
retrieved_docs.content: Smoking tobacco is the greatest risk factor for developing oral cancer due to carcinogenic chemicals, including nitrosamines, benzopyrenes, and aromatic amines. [3] The risk of developing oral cancer is 3 times higher in smokers compared with non-smokers. Individuals are also at risk from secondary passive smoking environments, particularly with chronic second-hand exposure. [4] The results of studies demonstrate a synergistic relationship with alcohol consumption, resulting in a higher risk of malignancy. [5]
